data.title: Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN) Shift To TTM Profitability Tests Bullish Narrative
data.description:
Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN) closed out FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$216.3 million and basic EPS of US$0.19, while trailing twelve month figures stood at US$765.7 million in revenue and EPS of US$0.46 as earnings moved into positive territory over the past year. The company reported quarterly revenue rising from US$187.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$216.3 million in Q4 2025, with basic EPS increasing from US$0.11 to US$0.19 over the same period, indicating firmer margins underpinning the latest results.

See our full analysis for Lumine Group.

With the headline numbers in place, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing stories about Lumine Group and assess where the data supports the narrative and where it starts to challenge it.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM net income swings from loss to US$118.8 million

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, net income moved from a loss of US$258.9 million in Q4 2024 to a profit of US$118.8 million in Q4 2025, with TTM basic EPS at US$0.46 over the same period.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is how the positive TTM earnings of US$118.8 million line up with quarterly net income of US$49.6 million in Q4 2025, which
    • supports the idea that profitability is now backed by several quarters of positive net income, including US$20.8 million, US$23.6 million and US$24.8 million across Q1 to Q3 2025, not just a single strong quarter,
    • yet also contrasts with the earlier TTM loss of US$1.8b in Q3 2024, so investors who take a bullish stance are still watching how durable this new level of profit proves to be over a longer window.

Stronger headline profits are only part of the picture, and many investors will want to see how this earnings shift ties back to the full company story before getting comfortable with it. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Lumine Group.

Revenue expansion outpaces market forecasts at 16.8%

  • Revenue growth is forecast at 16.8% per year compared with 6% per year for the broader Canadian market, and the latest TTM revenue sits at US$765.7 million versus US$668.4 million a year earlier.
  • Supporters of a bullish angle point to this faster revenue profile as a central part of the thesis, and the numbers give that view some clear backing because
    • quarterly revenue has stayed between US$178.7 million and US$216.3 million across 2025, which lines up with the idea of a larger revenue base feeding into the TTM figure of US$765.7 million,
    • while the move from a TTM net loss of US$258.9 million in Q4 2024 to a TTM profit of US$118.8 million suggests that higher revenue is now associated with earnings that are no longer in loss making territory.

Mixed valuation signals with 41.2x P/E and DCF fair value above price

  • Lumine Group is trading on a trailing P/E of 41.2x, which is lower than its peer average of 56.2x but higher than the North American Software industry at 28.2x, and its CA$26.05 share price is about 29.7% below a CA$37.04 DCF fair value estimate.
  • For more cautious investors, this combination of multiples and fair value estimates creates a clear tension, because
    • critics highlight that paying 41.2x earnings is above the broader software industry’s 28.2x, even though earnings growth of 8.48% per year is below the Canadian market forecast of 11.5% per year,
    • yet supporters may counter that the discount to the CA$37.04 DCF fair value and the shift from a TTM loss to US$118.8 million of TTM net income present a different angle than a simple P/E comparison on its own.

Next Steps

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Saia (SAIA) After Recent Less Than Truckload Focus?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Saia's current share price lines up with its underlying worth, you are not alone. This article is going to focus squarely on what you are paying versus what you might be getting.
  • Saia's stock closed at US$396.62, with a 1.9% decline over the last 7 days, a 6.0% return over 30 days, 17.6% year to date, 3.7% over 1 year, 41.3% over 3 years, and 82.3% over 5 years. This gives you a wide range of time frames to think about risk and reward.
  • Recent news coverage around Saia has focused on its role in the less than truckload sector, with investors watching how it positions itself within US transportation. This context has kept attention on how durable its business model might be as conditions change over time.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, Saia scores 1 out of 6. Next we will walk through the key valuation methods behind that score and then finish with a way of thinking about value that goes beyond the usual models.

Saia scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Saia Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth right now.

For Saia, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about $116.4 million. Analysts and model estimates point to free cash flow of $187.9 million in 2026, rising through the forecast period to an estimated $269.4 million in 2035, with figures beyond the analyst window extrapolated by Simply Wall St.

After discounting those projected cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $164.29 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $396.62, this implies the stock is 141.4% overvalued based on this DCF approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Saia.

Approach 2: Saia Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings, so it is a practical way to compare Saia with other stocks in the same space.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Saia currently trades on a P/E of 41.37x. That sits above the Transportation industry average of 36.39x and below the peer group average of 65.20x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Saia is 21.50x, which is their proprietary view of what Saia’s P/E might be given its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics. This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for company specific factors rather than relying on raw averages alone. Set against the current P/E of 41.37x, the Fair Ratio of 21.50x suggests Saia’s shares are pricing in a richer multiple than that framework would imply.

Result: OVERVALUED

  • Bears focus on this pattern, arguing that sizeable quarterly losses together with ongoing litigation and debt concerns keep pressure on cash flow and margins, even as forecasts point to earnings turning positive within three years.
    • The trailing 12 month figures show the company is still loss making, which matches the bearish view that legal and financing costs can weigh on reported profit even when the core business is working to improve.
    • At the same time, the move from a €963 million loss in Q3 to a €3.8b loss in Q4 gives critics more recent data to point to when they argue that earnings volatility has not yet settled.

  • Bulls argue that this revenue base, combined with new pharmaceuticals and agricultural products, can support a swing from a €3.6b loss today to meaningful profits over the next few years if margins improve as forecast.

data.title: PagSeguro Digital (NYSE:PAGS) Margin Decline Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:

These figures put fresh numbers on the table for investors watching its profitability profile. Over recent periods, the company has seen revenue move from R$5.0b in Q4 2024 to R$5.2b in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from R$1.93 to R$1.74. Trailing twelve month EPS stands at R$7.18 on revenue of R$19.7b and net income of R$2.1b, setting the context for how its margins are tracking through this latest print.

Margins Ease Back From 11.5% To 10.7%

  • Over the last 12 months, PagSeguro earned R$2.1b on R$19.7b of revenue, which works out to a 10.7% net margin compared with 11.5% a year earlier.
  • Bears argue that pressure from competition, Pix adoption and higher rates could keep squeezing profitability, and the recent move from 11.5% to 10.7% margin gives them support. However, trailing earnings of R$2.1b still line up with the more cautious view that margins ease rather than collapse.

Skeptics point to that 10.7% margin and slower recent growth as a warning sign. Yet they also track how the bear case could unfold in more detail in the 🐻 PagSeguro Digital Bear Case.

Five Year EPS Growth Versus Flat Recent Momentum

  • Over five years, earnings have grown about 14.3% a year, while one year earnings growth is around 0.09%, so the long run trend looks strong next to the recent flattening.
  • Bulls focus on that 14.3% yearly earnings growth and argue that business initiatives can sustain EPS progress, but the latest trailing EPS of R$7.18 and near flat 0.09% one year earnings growth mean the current data only partly backs that optimism.

Bulls point to that multi year EPS growth and a R$7.18 trailing EPS base as a starting point, and you can see how they build their case in the 🐂 PagSeguro Digital Bull Case.

Approach 1: Houlihan Lokey Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return required by shareholders, then converts that stream of excess profit into an estimated per share value.

For Houlihan Lokey, the starting point is its Book Value of $32.92 per share and a Stable Book Value estimate of $38.20 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 4 analysts. On these estimates, the business is expected to generate Stable EPS of $9.31 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from the same 4 analysts.

The model assumes a Cost of Equity of $3.02 per share. Subtracting that from the Stable EPS gives an Excess Return of $6.29 per share. This is consistent with an Average Return on Equity of 24.37%, which is the rate applied to the equity base to estimate those ongoing excess profits.

When those excess returns are capitalised, the model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $178.44 per share, which implies the stock is 11.8% undervalued relative to the recent price of $157.30.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Houlihan Lokey Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Houlihan Lokey, the P/E ratio is a useful shortcut because it links what you pay per share directly to the earnings that support that share price. It also conveniently reflects what the market is willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what investors expect from the business and how risky they think those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Houlihan Lokey currently trades on a P/E of 24.5x, compared with the Capital Markets industry average of 23.0x and a peer average of 15.8x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the company is 15.4x.
